Name: Is there a distinct category of dual for verbal agreement with a dual subject?
Comment: There is an agreement with the logical subject. In the comitative construction the agreement is dual (plural), however a few instances of singular, the verb is always in dual if the subject in a pronoun in dual. If there are two grammatical subjects (such as my mother and my father) in a sentence, the predicate is in the dual. This expresses that the two subjects act together. After quantifiers such as ŋukəgəə ‘many, much’, bǝnsǝ ‘all’ or numerals, the predicate agrees formally with the subject, i.e. it appears in the singular.

Name: Is non-agreement in number between the nominal subject and verbal predicate possible?
Comment: The predicate agrees in person and number with the subject.

Name: Can finite verbs agree in number with a nominal object?
Comment: Verbs in the objective conjugation agree with the object in number, the ending depends on the number. The verbal marker for object in dual is complex, it contains the dual marker.

Name: Can an adnominal property word agree with the noun in case?
Comment: Only the declination of three grammatical cases (nominative, accusative, genitive) can appear on adjectives. In all other cases, the pre-modifier is in the genitive case.

Name: Is there non-agreement between an adnominal property word and the head noun?
Comment: In all locative cases.

Name: Can adpositions be used to mark core verbal arguments?
Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (S/A) marking?
Comment: The subject of the temporal clause may be appear in the genitive.

Name: Can non-finite clauses use a special means for core argument (P) marking?
Do the characteristics of the object referent (topicality, animacy, definiteness) influence object marking?
Does object marking correlate with aspectuality?
Does object marking depend on the verb conjugation form?
Comment: With imperative forms the nominative is used.

Name: Is there differential subject marking?
Comment: In the active sentence, the over subject is always expressed with the nominative case.

Name: Can S and A be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Can S and P be conflated morphologically across clause boundaries, i.e., act as a syntactic pivot?
Is there grammatical marking of habituality?
Comment: The suffix -mUNHA shows that a situation takes place reguraly.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of frequentativity?
Is there grammatical marking of progressivity?
Comment: However, the imperfective verbs in the aorist tense refer to the present tense and thus express progressivity.

Name: Is it common to use a verb in the present tense for future time reference?
Is there grammatical marking of the immediate future?
Comment: The resultative is used to indicate that an action will start immediately.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of the pluperfect?
Can aspect be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Can tense be expressed overtly in the negative marker?
Comment: All tense markers can appear on the negative auxiliary.

Name: Is there grammatical marking of mirativity?
Comment: The dubitative mood may be used to express surprise. There is also an exclamative clitic that has a mirative usage.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of a desire or wish?
Comment: Optative may be used to express wishes or admonition. This mood is, however, rarely attested.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to irrealis?
Are verbs marked with a non-indicative mood in counterfactual conditional clauses?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic necessity?
Comment: There is necessitive (or debitive mood), but it is only seldom used.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of epistemic possibility?
Comment: There is a speculative mood that is used to express speaker's assumptions.

Name: Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of deontic possibility?
Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 3Sg?
Is there morphological marking on the verb used to direct commands/wishes, etc. to the 1Sg?
Is it common to use the imperative/jussive in complement clauses introduced by predicates expressing wish/desire/will?
Is there morphological marking on the verb dedicated to the expression of apprehension?
Is there a reflexive conjugation?
Can non-singular 2nd and 3rd person have the same person/number marking or morphosyntactic treatment?
Comment: Reflexive-medial conjugation shows the same person/number marking, e.g., ďeŋhiďi-Ɂi-ti is used for 2DU and 3DU (< ďembiɁsʲi 'to dress').

Name: Can the 3rd person plural verb form and impersonal (passive) verb form be encoded in the same way?
Comment: Passive sentences are formed using a passive suffix or a passive participle.

Name: Are 2nd person singular verb forms commonly used with a generic meaning?
Comment: In generic statement, normally the 2nd person possessive suffix is used as subject marker.

Name: Can an infinitival verb form be used in a directive function?
Can verbs in purposive subordinate clauses be infinitive forms?
Comment: Typically supine structures are used; the bare infinitive occurs with motion verbs.

Name: Are there sentences without overt verbal arguments?
Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the 3rd person singular forms of the present tense?
Is a copula commonly used for predicate nominals in the past tense?
Comment: Tense and mood markers only appear on verbal categories.

Name: Can locative constructions commonly occur without a copula?
Comment: If the pivot is expressed by a pronoun, copula is not obligatory.

Name: Is the same coordinating conjunction commonly used for NPs, VPs, and clauses?
Comment: Conjunctive coordinated clauses are usually juxtaposed without any coordinator. However, there are also some borrowed coordinators. The Russian coordinator is only used at the clause level.

Name: Does existential negation have a special negator?
Can standard negation be asymmetric in the present tense?
Can non-finite dependent clauses use the standard negation construction?
Is there a special negator that combines with non-finite forms?
Are there special negative non-finite forms?
Comment: The abessive participle.

Name: Is there an impersonal passive construction?
Comment: Passive structures can be formed from both transitive and intransitive verbs.

Name: Are there non-finite forms specializing in the relativization of particular core participants, e.g., subjects (S/A), direct objects (P), absolutive participants (S/P)?
Are there non-finite forms that can relativize all core participants (S/A/P) and possibly also some non-core participants?
Are there non-finite forms that can relativize at least some non-core participants but cannot relativize all core participants?
Is there a specialized negative participial form, which cannot be analyzed as a specific participial marker + negative marker?
Comment: The abessive participle refers to actions that have not yet occurred.

Name: Is there a future participle?
Comment: However, the necessitative participle has also been treated as a future participle.

Name: Are there participles that can encode their subject with possessive suffixes?
Are there participles that must agree with the nouns they modify in case and number in all contexts?
Comment: Participles show only partial agreement in case.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and action nominalizations?
Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and agentive nominalizations?
Comment: However, there is a separate suffix to indicate professioals, e.g. basu-Ɂsʲi ‘professional hunter’.

Name: Are there non-finite forms that can be used both as participles and converbs?
Is OV the neutral order in affirmative imperative clauses?
Is it possible to place an object between the negative marker and the main verb in imperative constructions?
Comment: The negative verb must precede the main verb, e.g., ŋanuďətə ŋəmsu ŋuəi-kalʲi-Ɂ ńi-Ɂ mətu-Ɂ [ordinary meat one-CAR-ADV NEGAUX-IMP.2SG cut-CNG] ‘Don’t cut an ordinary meat!’

Name: Is there diminutive (hypocoristic) marking on verbs?
Comment: However, there is a suffix that can be used in the attenuative meaning (i.e. to do something a bit).

Name: Can modifiers of action nominalizations in agent and patient roles both be encoded like possessors?
Can the modifier of action nominalizations in the patient role be encoded like a direct object?
Do core adjectives agree with the subject noun in number in the predicative position?
Is there a bound superlative degree marker on property words?
Comment: Analytic constructions are used.

Name: Can adpositions have person/number marking?
Are there adpositions that show a tripartite system?
Comment: E.g., lative: ńi ‘onto’, locative: ńini, elative: ńitə, prolative: ńimənɨ.

Name: Is there case compounding?
Are there morphological cases only used with pronouns?
Does the numeral modification of a noun have an effect on its case selection?
Is the singular form of the noun also used with numerals greater than 1?
Comment: Plural or dual is rarely used.

Name: Is there a dedicated case that expresses indirect object/recipient/goal (different from a local case)?
Comment: The lative is also used to express the recipient and the beneficiary.

Name: Is there a dedicated case that expresses a terminative meaning?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the absence of a referent (abessive)?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses motion along or by a referent (prolative)?
Comment: Prolative is used to mark direction.

Name: Is there a locative case that expresses goal?
Is there a locative case that expresses location?
Is there a locative case that expresses source?
Is there case syncretism for goal and location marking?
Is there case syncretism for location and source marking?
Are there two sets of local cases?
Are there three or more sets of local cases?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the means by which something is carried out (instrumental) and/or accompaniment (comitative)?
Comment: The suffix has been recently developed from the lative postposition na.

Name: Is there a case that expresses the means by which something is carried out (instrumental), which is different from a case that expresses accompaniment (comitative) or other relations?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses the result of a change of state (translative) and/or a state of being (essive)?
Is there a case that expresses a state of being (essive), which is different from a case that expresses the result of a change of state (translative) or other relations?
Is there a dedicated case that expresses equativity?
Is there an inflectional nominal marker for expressing benefactive or destinative semantics independent from the dative?
Is there (in addition to the basic declension) a declension different from a possessive declension?
Is it possible to use the singular with the paired body parts/clothing/accessories that accompany them?
Comment: With paired body parts, dual is typically used. In modern texts also plural can be found.

Name: Can the marking of the possessor in the attributive construction vary?
Comment: The non-pronominal possessor appears in the genitive case, whereas the pronominal possessor occurs in the nominative case (see also UT102).

Name: Can the possessor in the attributive construction be encoded with the nominative?
Comment: The nominative form is used with pronouns.

Name: Are singular and plural possessee distinguished in the possessive declension?
Comment: The possessive suffixes refer to the person and number of the possessor but also the number of the possessed.

Name: Can a 2Sg possessive suffix be used to mark definiteness?
Comment: The referential use of 2Sg possessive suffix may occur only when the marked NP occurs in the subject position (see also UT105).

Name: Can a 3Sg possessive suffix be used to mark definiteness?
Comment: Possessive suffixes can be used in the referential function that is similar to the definite article but it cannot be argued that the language has definite articles.

Name: Is there a human vs. non-human distinction in independent interrogative pronouns?
Comment: The distinction is made between humans vs. non-humans. However, in certain cases, 'what' can also be used for a human referent.

Name: Does 'who' commonly appear as a relative pronoun used for animates other than humans?
Comment: There are no relative pronouns.

Name: Does 'who' commonly appear as a relative pronoun used for inanimates?
Comment: There are no relative pronouns.

Name: Can a demonstrative stem be used as a pronoun with a 3Sg human referent?
Are there possessive suffixes, the position of which varies in relation to case/number suffixes?
Are case and number marked cumulatively across the paradigm?
Comment: In plural, the number marker and the case suffix are fused.

Name: Are there different words for mother’s brother and father’s brother?
Are there different words for younger and older relatives for relations other than siblings?
Is there an animacy distinction for both concepts: 'new/young' and 'old'?
Is there a distinction for both 'warm' and 'cold' depending on whether reference is to weather or the temperature of objects?
Is there contrastive length for consonants?
Is there contrastive length for vowels?
Comment: There is a binary opposition regarding vowel length; phonologically long vowels constitute two syllables.

Name: Is there a contrast between short and long unstressed vowels?
Is there a contrast in the length of geminate consonants?
Are there more than two phonological quantities?
Are there extralong syllables?
Comment: The maximal syllable is CV͡VC; any vowel or diphtong can appear in the nucleus position.

Name: Is the main foot structure trochaic?
Is there lexically distinctive word stress?
Is there morphologically conditioned stress?
Is there fixed primary stress?
Comment: Primary stress falls on the penultimate syllable.

Name: Is there stress in heavy syllables?
Comment: However, words ending in long vowels or vowel sequences trigger stress.

Name: Is there non-initial primary stress?
Comment: It is possible if a word is trisyllabic or longer.

Name: Is there palatal vowel harmony?
Comment: Occurs with limitations.

Name: Is there velar vowel harmony?
Comment: Occurs with limitations.

Name: Is there labial vowel harmony?
Comment: Occurs with limitations.

Name: Are there closing diphthongs?
Are there opening diphthongs?
Comment: Two diphthongs that have phonemic status: /i͡a/ and /u͡a/. However, they have also been interpreted as monophthongs (see Janhunen and Gruzdeva 2020).

Name: Is there a qualitative contrast between subsets of short and long vowels?
Are there full vs. reduced vowels as contrastive subsets?
Is there a high front rounded vowel /y/?
Is there a mid-high front rounded vowel /ø/?
Is there a high back unrounded vowel /ɯ ɨ/?
Is there a mid-high back unrounded vowel /ɤ ɘ/?
Is there a low back rounded vowel /ɒ/?
Is there a contrast between mid front vowels (/e/ or /ɛ/) and low front vowels (/æ/ or /a/)?
Are there word-initial consonant clusters?
Are there word-internal consonant clusters in the syllable onset?
Are there consonant clusters in the syllable coda?
Are there word-internal intervocalic clusters of three or more consonants?
Are there word-final clusters of three or more consonants?
Are there palatalized alveolar consonants?
Are there palatalized labial and velar consonants?
Is there a palatal stop?
Comment: The palatal stop [c] occurs as an allophone of /t͡ʃ/.

Name: Is there a palatal nasal /ɲ/?
Is there a palatal lateral /ʎ/?
Is there a velar nasal /ŋ/?
Is there an alveolar affricate /t͡s/?
Is there a non-alveolar affricate (e.g., /t͡ʃ/, /t͡ɕ/)?
Is there a phonological contrast between an unvoiced alveolar sibilant /s/ and an unvoiced postalveolar sibilant /ʃ/?
Are there voiced plosives (e.g., /b g d/)?
Comment: Only /b/ and /g/, whereas /d/ appears as an allophone of /nt/ and in some Russians loanwords. However, see UT162 for a comment by Janhunen and Gruzdeva (2020).

Name: Is there a glottal stop /ʔ/?
Is there a phonological contrast between plain and labial velar consonants (e.g., /k/ vs. /kʷ/)?
Is there a constraint on the word-initial alveolar trill /r/?
Comment: /Ɂ/, /j/, and /r/ are not allowed in word-initial position.

Name: Is there a laryngeal fricative /h/?
Is there a voiced velar fricative /ɣ/?
Is there a voiceless velar fricative /x/?
Is there a voiced dental fricative /ð/?
Comment: However, according to Janhunen and Gruzdeva (2020), the intervocalic spirant [ð] and the postnasal voiced stop [d] represent allophones of a single phoneme that could  be rendered as /d/.

Name: Is there a labio-velar approximant /w/?
Is there a voiceless labiodental fricative /f/?
Is there radical qualitative consonant gradation?
Is there quantitative consonant gradation?
Comment: No proper consonant geminates at all.
